programs.name: Idaho Medicaid Aged and Disabled Waiver
programs.tagline: If your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, or medication but wants to stay home, this program covers care services that would otherwise cost thousands monthly.

localEntities.name: Statewide via Idaho Department of Health and Welfare

programs.intro:
If your parent is 65+ or disabled and needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay home, Idaho's Aged and Disabled Waiver can pay for home care services instead. The program covers attendant care, homemaker services, adult day health, respite care, home-delivered meals, and medical equipment — services that typically cost $2,000–5,000 per month out of pocket.

To qualify, your parent must meet strict financial limits (assets under $2,000 for single applicants) and pass a clinical assessment proving they need daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, mobility, eating, or toileting. Because this is a Medicaid waiver program, it also covers all regular medical expenses — doctor visits, prescriptions, hospital care — with no copays or deductibles.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need nursing home-level care, determined through clinical assessment of daily living activities like bathing, dressing, mobility, eating, and toileting. Cognitive impairments also considered.
otherRequirements: Must qualify for Aid to the Aged, Blind, and Disabled (AABD) Medicaid

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through Idaho's Medicaid system, then complete a separate clinical assessment to determine care needs.

steps.title: Apply for Medicaid
steps.description: Submit application online at Idaho Department of Health and Welfare website for Medicaid for Elderly or Disabled Adults

steps.title: Financial eligibility review
steps.description: Department reviews income, assets, and AABD Medicaid qualification

steps.title: Clinical assessment
steps.description: If financially eligible, complete Uniform Assessment Instrument (UAI) to determine if your parent needs nursing facility level of care

steps.title: Care plan development
steps.description: If approved, work with case manager to develop individualized service plan

applicationGuide.tip: Gather all financial documents before starting — the Look-Back Rule examines any asset transfers in the past 60 months, which could create penalty periods.

contentSections.heading: Asset Limits: What Counts
included: Real estate other than primary home
Life insurance with cash value over $1,500

categories.name: Identity & Residency
items: Proof of Idaho residency (utility bill, lease)

contentSections.text: The Look-Back Rule examines any asset transfers in the 60 months before applying. If your parent gave away money or property below fair market value during this period, it could create a penalty period where they're temporarily ineligible for benefits.

Your parent must qualify for regular AABD Medicaid first, then pass the nursing facility level of care assessment. Meeting the financial requirements doesn't guarantee approval — the clinical assessment is equally important.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house if she qualifies?
faqs.answer: Yes, as long as the home equity is under $752,000 (2026 limit) and she lives there or intends to return. The house won't count toward the $2,000 asset limit.

faqs.question: What happens if she's in the hospital when we apply?
faqs.answer: She can still apply and be assessed. The clinical evaluation will determine if she needs nursing home-level care regardless of where she's currently receiving treatment.

faqs.question: Does this cover everything regular Medicaid covers too?
faqs.answer: Yes. Because this is a Medicaid program, it covers all standard medical expenses — doctor visits, prescriptions, hospital care — plus the additional home and community-based services.

faqs.question: What if her income is just over the AABD limit?
faqs.answer: She may still qualify through spend-down provisions or other Medicaid pathways. Income limits vary by marital status, so contact Idaho Medicaid directly to review her specific situation.

faqs.question: How much care can she actually get through this program?
faqs.answer: Services are individualized based on her assessed needs. The program covers attendant care, homemaker services, adult day health, respite care, meals, transportation, and medical equipment — whatever is needed to safely remain at home instead of entering a nursing facility.

faqs.question: Can she have other insurance along with this?
faqs.answer: Yes, but Medicaid typically becomes the secondary payer. Any other insurance she has will be coordinated with her Medicaid benefits.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.tagline: All medical care under one team — no deductibles or copays if your parent qualifies for both Medicare and Medicaid.

localEntities.name: Limited to PACE organizations in Idaho service areas; no specific providers listed

programs.intro:
If your parent is 55+ and needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay in their community, PACE provides all their medical care through one coordinated team. Because PACE covers all Medicare and Medicaid services under one program, your parent won't need to coordinate between separate doctors, specialists, or insurance plans — one interdisciplinary team manages everything from primary care to prescription drugs to transportation.

Unlike most programs, PACE has no income or asset limits. Your parent qualifies based on clinical need (requiring nursing home-level care) and geography (living in a PACE service area). If your parent has both Medicare and Medicaid, all services come with no deductibles or copays. If they only have Medicare, they pay a monthly premium.

PACE availability is extremely limited in Idaho. Your parent must live in a specific service area where a PACE organization operates, and these programs may have waitlists due to enrollment caps.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be certified by Idaho as needing nursing home level care (a clinical assessment of whether your parent needs daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or other activities of daily living), but able to live safely in the community with PACE services
otherRequirements: Must reside in a PACE-specific service area in Idaho
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage, hospice, or certain Medicare drug plans

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local Idaho PACE provider directly for an in-person assessment — processing time varies by location.

steps.title: Find your local PACE provider
steps.description: Search for PACE organizations operating in your parent's area in Idaho. Not all areas have PACE providers.

steps.title: Contact the provider directly
steps.description: Call the PACE organization to request an assessment. Each provider handles their own enrollment process.

steps.title: Complete in-person assessment
steps.description: PACE staff will evaluate your parent's need for nursing home-level care and develop a personalized care plan.

steps.title: Get state certification
steps.description: Idaho must certify that your parent meets Nursing Facility Level of Care requirements if not already documented.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Not specified; depends on local program
applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible due to limited spaces; varies by location
applicationGuide.tip: Call as soon as possible — PACE programs often have waitlists due to enrollment caps.

categories.name: Health Information
items: State NFLOC certification if already available

categories.name: Residency
items: Proof of address in PACE service area

contentSections.text: PACE is not available everywhere in Idaho. Your parent must live in a specific service area where a PACE organization operates, and Idaho has very limited PACE programs.
Once enrolled in PACE, your parent cannot use other plans like Medicare Advantage or hospice. PACE becomes their sole provider for all Medicare and Medicaid services.

faqs.question: What if my parent's income is too high for Medicaid but they need PACE services?
faqs.answer: Your parent can still enroll in PACE with Medicare only, but they'll pay a monthly premium for the Medicaid-covered services. There are no income or asset limits for PACE eligibility.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep seeing her current doctor if she enrolls in PACE?
faqs.answer: No. PACE becomes your parent's sole healthcare provider. She'll get a new primary care doctor and interdisciplinary team through the PACE program who coordinate all her care.

faqs.question: What happens if we move to a different city in Idaho?
faqs.answer: If you move outside the PACE service area, your parent will have to disenroll from PACE. PACE is only available in specific geographic areas, and Idaho has very limited coverage.

faqs.question: How is this different from regular Medicare and Medicaid?
faqs.answer: Instead of coordinating between multiple doctors and insurance plans, PACE provides all services through one team at one location. Your parent gets primary care, specialists, prescriptions, transportation, and social services all coordinated together.

faqs.question: What if the PACE center is far from our house?
faqs.answer: PACE provides transportation to and from the day center and medical appointments as part of the program. This is included in your parent's care plan.

faqs.question: Can my parent leave PACE if they don't like it?
faqs.answer: Yes, your parent can disenroll from PACE at any time and return to regular Medicare and Medicaid coverage. However, they may need to find new healthcare providers.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)
programs.tagline: Help pay your parent's Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copays — potential savings of $2,000+ per year.

localEntities.name: Local Idaho Department of Health and Welfare offices (statewide)

programs.intro:
If your parent struggles to afford Medicare costs, three programs can help pay premiums, deductibles, and copays. The Qualified Medicare Beneficiary (QMB) program covers all Medicare out-of-pocket costs. The Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary (SLMB) and Qualifying Individual (QI) programs pay Medicare Part B premiums ($185/month in 2026).

These programs have strict income limits — roughly $1,350-$1,823/month for a single person depending on the program tier. Because they're administered through Idaho Medicaid, your parent must also meet asset limits (under $9,950 for singles, $14,910 for couples). The QI program operates first-come, first-served with limited federal funding, so timing matters.
programs.savingsSource: Part B premiums ($2,220/year) plus deductibles and copays for QMB

otherRequirements: Must be eligible for Medicare Part A

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online through Idaho's Medicaid portal or call 1-877-456-1233 — processing takes up to 45 days.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: You'll need proof of income (Social Security statements, pay stubs), bank statements, Medicare card, and ID.

steps.title: Choose application method
steps.description: Apply online at healthandwelfare.idaho.gov, call 1-877-456-1233, or visit your local Idaho Department of Health and Welfare office.

steps.title: Submit complete application
steps.description: Include all documentation with your application to avoid delays. Incomplete applications will be returned.

steps.title: Follow up if needed
steps.description: Idaho has 45 days to process your application. Benefits start the first day of the month after approval.

applicationGuide.waitlist: QI program operates first-come, first-served with limited federal funding — may have waitlist
applicationGuide.tip: Apply for QI early in the year when funding resets — priority goes to people who had it the previous year.

tiers.name: QMB (Qualified Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.coverage: Pays Medicare Part A premiums, Part B premiums, deductibles, coinsurance, and copayments. Providers cannot balance bill you.

tiers.name: SLMB (Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.coverage: Pays Medicare Part B premiums only ($185/month in 2026). Automatically qualifies for Extra Help prescription coverage.

tiers.name: QI (Qualifying Individual)
tiers.coverage: Pays Medicare Part B premiums only. Automatically qualifies for Extra Help prescription coverage with low copays (≤$12.65 per drug).

contentSections.text: QI program funding is limited and operates first-come, first-served. Apply early in the year and reapply annually by the deadline to maintain coverage.
Income and asset limits update every April based on federal poverty levels. If your parent was just over the limit last year, they might qualify now with updated thresholds.

faqs.question: Can Mom keep her house and still qualify?
faqs.answer: Yes. Your parent's primary home doesn't count toward the asset limit, regardless of value. They can also keep one car, household goods, life insurance, and burial plots.

faqs.question: What if Dad's income is just over the QMB limit but under SLMB?
faqs.answer: He can still apply for SLMB or QI, which have higher income limits. SLMB covers Part B premiums ($185/month) and both programs automatically qualify him for Extra Help prescription coverage.

faqs.question: Does this affect Mom's other benefits like Social Security?
faqs.answer: No. Medicare Savings Programs don't reduce Social Security or other benefits. They're designed to work alongside Medicare to reduce healthcare costs.

faqs.question: How long does coverage last once approved?
faqs.answer: QMB and SLMB coverage continues as long as your parent meets eligibility requirements. QI requires annual reapplication and operates on limited funding, so reapply early each year.

faqs.question: Can providers still charge Dad extra fees if he has QMB?
faqs.answer: No. QMB protects against balance billing — providers who accept Medicare cannot charge your parent anything beyond what Medicare pays. This is a major protection other programs don't offer.

faqs.question: What happens if the QI program runs out of funding?
faqs.answer: Your parent would go on a waitlist for the following year. Priority goes to people who had QI coverage the previous year, so it's important to reapply early and annually.
